title: HP's Consumer PC Business Will Shrink
author: Peter S. Kastner
source_file: 2002 HP_s Consumer PC Business Will Shrink PSK.pdf
abstract: Following the HP-Compaq merger rationale that combined volume would yield better PC margins, Aberdeen research challenges this assumption directly: post-merger HP's PC unit volumes will actually be less than the sum of separate Compaq and HP levels. This InSight examines how HP is likely to fare in business and retail/consumer PC markets following the merger, predicting consumer PC business contraction rather than the expansion promised by HP management.
importance_rationale: Written precisely at the time of the HP-Compaq merger closing, this contrarian Aberdeen view directly challenged HP's stated merger rationale and reached a concrete prediction about consumer PC volumes. It was a notable piece of independent analysis on the most controversial tech merger of 2002.
relevance_rationale: The specific HP-Compaq merger is historical, but the analytical framework — questioning merger volume synergy assumptions in commodity hardware markets — remains applicable to current M&A analysis. The consumer PC market structure analysis is dated.
prescience_rationale: Aberdeen correctly identified that post-merger HP would not achieve volume growth from the merger in consumer PCs: Dell overtook HP+Compaq as PC market share leader in early 2003. However HP did eventually recover PC market leadership by 2004 under Carly Fiorina and later Mark Hurd, making the prediction partially correct in the short term.
